sections.stepTitle: Order at a Korean café like a pro

scenes.text: Ordering your first drink at a Korean café — Americanos are the most popular coffee in Korea!
The cashier asks how many — 하나 (ha-na) = one. You've got this.
The same pattern works for any item — just swap the item name and number.

sections.stepTitle: Korean has two number systems — and you'll use both!
sections.insight: For counting items (1, 2, 3...) Korean uses native numbers: 하나(1), 둘(2), 셋(3). For money, floors, and minutes, it uses Sino-Korean: 일(1), 이(2), 삼(3). You'll learn both naturally through practice!

sections.instruction: Add these to your order — just say it after the drink name! 👇

questions.question: How do you order "One Americano, please"?
questions.correctMsg: Perfect! 아메리카노 하나 주세요 — you can order anything by swapping the item and number.
questions.wrongMsg: Not quite! The answer is 아메리카노 하나 주세요. 주세요 = 'please give me' — the magic ending for ordering anything!

questions.question: How do you say "iced, please" at a café?
questions.sub: Think back to the pattern practice!
questions.correctMsg: Correct! 아이스 is borrowed directly from English — 'ice' → 아이스. Easy!
questions.wrongMsg: Not quite! 아이스 주세요 (a-i-seu ju-se-yo) — 아이스 comes from the English word 'ice'!

sections.stepTitle: Seoul has the most cafés per capita in the world!

culture.title: Korea's café culture is next level
culture.body:
Seoul has more cafés per square kilometer than almost anywhere on earth. 카공족 (ka-gong-jok) — people who study or work at cafés — are a real cultural phenomenon.

Popular order tips:
• Say "아이스 주세요" for iced (most Koreans drink iced year-round!)
• "따뜻하게 주세요" = hot/warm, please
• "테이크아웃 주세요" = to go, please (takeout = 테이크아웃)

Tip: many Korean cafés are incredibly Instagram-worthy. Themed cafés, rooftop cafés, and dessert cafés are everywhere!

drama.japanese: "Shall we go to a café together?" / "Yeah, let's go!"
drama.note: A classic K-drama scene. Now you can have this conversation! ✨
